title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: Estimating mortality humanitarian crises one challenging problems epidemiology. active conflicts disasters, systems normally collect mortality data — health facilities, civil registration, surveillance networks — often disrupted destroyed. creates paradox: mortality highest precisely ability measure lowest. nurah package developed address challenge simulation-based approach. simulating realistic crisis data known “true” mortality, can evaluate well different statistical methods recover truth various conditions data missingness bias. package provides tools : Define causal structures using directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) based frameworks like Checchi et al. (2017) Simulate crisis data realistic mortality outcomes (negative binomial counts) Model data missingness different assumptions (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) Fit Bayesian hierarchical models handle missing data via Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW) imputation Evaluate estimator performance simulation studies vignette demonstrates complete workflow using simulation study design relevant Gaza crisis context.

title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: can simulate data, need define causal relationships crisis conditions mortality. package implements causal framework Checchi et al. (2017), provides comprehensive map crisis exposures lead excess deaths intermediate health domains. framework recognises deaths crises occur multiple pathways: direct violence, also disrupted healthcare, food insecurity leading malnutrition, displacement causing disease outbreaks, mental health impacts. pathways operates different timescales different magnitudes. checchi_2017_dag() function creates DAG structure, dummy_checchi_2017_parameters() provides plausible effect sizes time lags causal pathway. real application, parameters calibrated empirical estimates literature. output tells nurah_dag object 30 directed edges connecting various health domains ultimately population mortality.
code: # Load the Checchi et al. (2017) DAG with dummy parameters dag <- checchi_2017_dag(parameters = dummy_checchi_2017_parameters())  # Examine the structure cat("DAG class:", class(dag), "\n") ## DAG class: nurah_dag cat("Number of edges:", nrow(dag$parameters), "\n") ## Number of edges: 30

text: edge DAG two key parameters: Effect size: magnitude causal effect (much one-unit change parent variable affects child variable) Lag: time delay (days) effect manifests table shows parameters edges directly affect population mortality. effect sizes arbitrary scale dummy parameters, calibrated model represent contributions crude mortality rate. Direct causes population mortality largest effects come epidemic occurrence burden endemic infectious diseases, reflecting reality communicable diseases often leading cause excess mortality crises. title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: Now defined causal structure, can simulate synthetic crisis data. simulate_crisis_data() function generates panel data governorate-month level, simulating values node DAG according specified causal relationships. key parameters : dag: causal structure defined start_date n_periods: temporal extent simulation resolution: time unit output (e.g., “month” governorate-month data) spatial_structure: Names number spatial units (governorates) initial_population: Starting population per governorate noise_level: Controls stochastic variation (1 = full randomness, 0 = deterministic) mortality_node: DAG node represents mortality outcome mortality_phi: Overdispersion parameter negative binomial mortality mortality_intercept: Baseline log-rate mortality output data frame 120 rows (5 governorates × 12 months) columns node DAG plus administrative identifiers. structure mirrors format real governorate-level health data. following provides summary statistics simulated mortality: Simulation summary numbers represent ground truth simulation — actual mortality occurred. real crisis, never know values certainty; goal estimate accurately possible incomplete data.
code:
# Simulate 5 governorates over 12 months sim_data <- simulate_crisis_data(   dag = dag,   start_date = "2023-10-01",   n_periods = 12,   resolution = "month",   spatial_structure = paste0("gov_", sprintf("%02d", 1:5)),   initial_population = 100000,   noise_level = 1,   mortality_node = "Population mortality",   mortality_phi = 1,   mortality_intercept = -8 )  # Examine output structure cat("Dimensions:", nrow(sim_data), "rows ×", ncol(sim_data), "columns
") ## Dimensions: 60 rows × 22 columns cat("Governorates:", length(unique(sim_data$region)), "
") ## Governorates: 5 cat("Time periods:", length(unique(sim_data$date)), "
") ## Time periods: 12 # Summary of key variables sim_summary <- sim_data %>%   summarise(     total_deaths = sum(`Population mortality`, na.rm = TRUE),     mean_monthly_deaths = mean(`Population mortality`, na.rm = TRUE),     sd_monthly_deaths = sd(`Population mortality`, na.rm = TRUE),     total_population = sum(population, na.rm = TRUE) / 12,     .groups = "drop"   )  knitr::kable(sim_summary, caption = "Simulation summary", digits = 0)

title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: Understanding mortality simulated helps interpret results. package uses negative binomial distribution, appropriate count data exhibits overdispersion (variance greater mean). typical mortality data, clustering unmeasured heterogeneity cause variability simple Poisson model predict. statistical model : Yit∼NegBin(μit,ϕ)Y_{} \sim \text{NegBin}(\mu_{}, \phi) : log(μit)=log(Nit)+ηit\log(\mu_{}) = \log(N_{}) + \eta_{}ηit=α+∑jβj⋅Xj,+ui\eta_{} = \alpha + \sum_j \beta_j \cdot X_{j,} + u_i components : YitY_{}: Death count governorate ii time tt NitN_{}: Population risk (used offset model rates rather counts) α\alpha: Baseline log-rate (default: -8, corresponds ~0.03 deaths per 1,000 per day, roughly 1 per 1,000 per month — plausible baseline mortality rate) βj\beta_j: Effect parent node jj DAG uiu_i: Governorate-level random effect (captures unmeasured spatial heterogeneity) ϕ\phi: Overdispersion parameter (smaller values = overdispersion) following plots show distribution simulated mortality counts trajectories time governorate:  histogram shows right-skewed distribution typical count data, trajectory plot reveals temporal trends (shared across governorates) spatial heterogeneity (differences governorates given time).
code: # Visualize mortality distribution p1 <- ggplot(sim_data, aes(x = `Population mortality`)) +   geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.7, color = "white") +   labs(     title = "Distribution of Monthly Mortality Counts",     x = "Deaths per governorate-month",     y = "Frequency"   )  p2 <- ggplot(sim_data, aes(x = date, y = `Population mortality`,                              group = region, color = region)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.7) +   labs(     title = "Mortality Trajectories by Governorate",     x = "Date",     y = "Monthly deaths",     color = "Governorate"   ) +   theme(legend.position = "right")  p1 + p2

title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: Let’s start simplest case: Missing Completely Random. MCAR, observation probability missing, regardless variables. rarely realistic crisis settings, serves baseline comparison. First, identify predictor variables exist simulated data: pi_base = 0.7, expect approximately 30% observations missing variable, indeed ’s see. MCAR, missingness rates roughly equal across variables.
code: # Identify available predictors predictors <- c(   "Nutritional status",   "Burden of endemic infectious diseases",   "Burden of NCDs",   "Epidemic occurrence and severity" ) available_preds <- intersect(predictors, names(sim_data))  # Apply MCAR missingness (baseline) mcar_result <- simulate_missingness(   data = sim_data,   predictors = available_preds,   regime = "MCAR",   pi_base = 0.7  # 70% of observations retained )  sim_mcar <- mcar_result$data  # Check missingness rates mcar_rates <- sapply(available_preds, function(p) mean(is.na(sim_mcar[[p]]))) cat("MCAR missingness rates:\n") ## MCAR missingness rates: print(round(mcar_rates * 100, 1)) ##                    Nutritional status Burden of endemic infectious diseases  ##                                    25                                    30  ##                        Burden of NCDs      Epidemic occurrence and severity  ##                                    25                                    35

title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: realistic scenario Missing Random (MAR), observation probability depends observed crisis indicators. intuition data collection harder areas active conflict high displacement — can observe conditions data sources (e.g., ACLED conflict events, displacement tracking). specify observation probability decreases conflict intensity (“Exposure armed attacks”) displacement high: negative weights mean higher values conflict displacement decrease observation probability. overall missingness rates may differ MCAR now depend distribution driver variables. Importantly, MAR can potentially correct bias using methods like Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW), variables driving missingness observed.
code: # MAR: missingness depends on conflict intensity mar_result <- simulate_missingness(   data = sim_data,   predictors = available_preds,   regime = "MAR",   pi_base = 0.7,   mar_drivers = c("Exposure to armed attacks or mechanical force of nature",                   "Forced displacement"),   mar_weights = c(     "Exposure to armed attacks or mechanical force of nature" = -0.5,     "Forced displacement" = -0.3   ) )  sim_mar <- mar_result$data  # Check missingness rates mar_rates <- sapply(available_preds, function(p) mean(is.na(sim_mar[[p]]))) cat("MAR missingness rates:\n") ## MAR missingness rates: print(round(mar_rates * 100, 1)) ##                    Nutritional status Burden of endemic infectious diseases  ##                                  35.0                                  40.0  ##                        Burden of NCDs      Epidemic occurrence and severity  ##                                  28.3                                  30.0

text: challenging scenario Missing Random (MNAR), probability observing variable depends unobserved value . example, severe malnutrition cases may less likely recorded health facilities overwhelmed precisely conditions deteriorate. MNAR mechanism adds term ω⋅Xitk\omega \cdot X_{itk} observation model, XitkX_{itk} (potentially unobserved) value variable. negative ω\omega means larger values (e.g., worse outcomes) less likely observed. MNAR, neither complete case analysis IPW can fully correct bias, mechanism depends values observe. option sensitivity analysis — exploring estimates change different assumptions MNAR parameter ω\omega.
code: # MNAR: worse values are less likely to be observed mnar_result <- simulate_missingness(   data = sim_data,   predictors = available_preds,   regime = "MNAR",   pi_base = 0.7,   mar_drivers = c("Exposure to armed attacks or mechanical force of nature"),   mar_weights = c("Exposure to armed attacks or mechanical force of nature" = -0.3),   mnar_omega = -0.6  # Negative: worse outcomes less likely observed )  sim_mnar <- mnar_result$data  # Check missingness rates mnar_rates <- sapply(available_preds, function(p) mean(is.na(sim_mnar[[p]]))) cat("MNAR missingness rates:\n") ## MNAR missingness rates: print(round(mnar_rates * 100, 1)) ##                    Nutritional status Burden of endemic infectious diseases  ##                                  38.3                                  25.0  ##                        Burden of NCDs      Epidemic occurrence and severity  ##                                  21.7                                  26.7

title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: understand missingness varies across time variables regime, can create heatmaps showing proportion missing values:  MCAR, heatmap show relatively uniform missingness across time variables. MAR MNAR, one may see temporal patterns (missingness conflict intense) potentially variable-specific patterns (indicators harder collect others).
code: # Create missingness indicator dataset create_miss_df <- function(data, regime_name) {   data %>%     select(region, date, all_of(available_preds)) %>%     pivot_longer(cols = all_of(available_preds),                   names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%     mutate(       missing = is.na(value),       regime = regime_name     ) }  miss_comparison <- bind_rows(   create_miss_df(sim_mcar, "MCAR"),   create_miss_df(sim_mar, "MAR"),   create_miss_df(sim_mnar, "MNAR") )  # Missingness heatmap by regime miss_summary <- miss_comparison %>%   group_by(regime, date, variable) %>%   summarise(miss_rate = mean(missing), .groups = "drop")  ggplot(miss_summary, aes(x = date, y = variable, fill = miss_rate)) +   geom_tile() +   facet_wrap(~regime, ncol = 1) +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "#c0392b",                        labels = scales::percent) +   labs(     title = "Missingness Patterns by Regime",     subtitle = "MCAR shows uniform missingness; MAR/MNAR show systematic patterns",     x = "Month",     y = "Variable",     fill = "Missing\nRate"   ) +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: addition simulated missingness mechanisms , package can apply real-world missingness patterns observed data. particularly useful calibrating simulations match actual data availability specific crisis. apply_mask() function takes data frame specifying exactly observations missing. create mask mimics reporting collapse observed Gaza, northern areas lost health data collection earlier crisis southern areas:  pattern reflects health system capacity typically degrades conflict zones — starting areas closest active fighting spreading outward time. applying mask simulated data, can evaluate well estimators perform realistic data availability constraints.
code: # Create a Gaza-style mask (reporting collapses over time, especially in north) governorates <- unique(sim_data$region) months <- 1:12  gaza_mask <- expand.grid(   gov = governorates,   month = months,   var = available_preds[1],   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) %>%   mutate(     # Northern governorates (1-3) lose reporting after month 3     # Central governorates (4-7) lose reporting after month 6     # Southern governorates (8-10) maintain partial reporting     is_north = gov %in% governorates[1:3],     is_central = gov %in% governorates[4:7],     observed = case_when(       is_north & month > 3 ~ 0L,       is_central & month > 6 ~ 0L,       month > 9 ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.3),       TRUE ~ 1L     )   ) %>%   select(gov, month, var, observed)  # Apply the mask sim_masked <- apply_mask(sim_data, gaza_mask, mode = "force")  # Visualize the mask pattern mask_viz <- gaza_mask %>%   mutate(gov = factor(gov, levels = rev(governorates)))  ggplot(mask_viz, aes(x = month, y = gov, fill = factor(observed))) +   geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = "#e74c3c", "1" = "#2ecc71"),                     labels = c("Missing", "Observed")) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:12) +   labs(     title = "Gaza-Style Reporting Collapse Pattern",     subtitle = "Northern areas lose reporting earlier in the crisis",     x = "Month",     y = "Governorate",     fill = "Status"   )

code: # Extract coefficient estimates extract_coefs <- function(fit, model_name) {   fe <- fixef(fit)   data.frame(     model = model_name,     parameter = rownames(fe),     estimate = fe[, "Estimate"],     lower = fe[, "Q2.5"],     upper = fe[, "Q97.5"],     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   ) }  coef_comparison <- bind_rows(   extract_coefs(fit_cc, "Complete Case"),   extract_coefs(fit_ipw, "IPW"),   extract_coefs(fit_oracle, "Oracle") ) %>%   filter(parameter != "Intercept")  ggplot(coef_comparison, aes(x = estimate, y = parameter, color = model)) +   geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper),                   position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +   geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(     title = "Coefficient Estimates by Model",     subtitle = "Points show posterior means; lines show 95% credible intervals",     x = "Estimate (95% CI)",     y = "Parameter",     color = "Model"   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("Complete Case" = "#e74c3c",                                   "IPW" = "#3498db",                                   "Oracle" = "#2ecc71"))

Posterior predictive checks compare distribution observed outcomes distribution outcomes predicted model:  good model produce predictions (light lines) closely match observed data (dark line). Systematic discrepancies might indicate model misspecification.
code: # Posterior predictive check for IPW model pp_check(fit_ipw, type = "dens_overlay", ndraws = 50) +   labs(title = "Posterior Predictive Check (IPW Model)",        subtitle = "Dark line = observed data; light lines = model predictions")

title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: key question mortality estimation : close estimates truth? simulation study, luxury knowing true values, can directly compute bias performance metrics. ’ll compute: Bias: Estimated - True (positive = overestimate, negative = underestimate) Relative Bias: Bias / True × 100% Coverage: 95% credible interval contain true value? Model Performance Metrics Interpretation: Negative bias indicates underestimation (common complete case MAR) Relative bias contextualises magnitude — 10% bias may acceptable, 50% Coverage = 1 means true value fell within 95% CI (want happen ~95% time across replicates)
code: # True total deaths true_total <- sum(sim_data[["Population mortality"]], na.rm = TRUE)  # Function to compute metrics for a model get_metrics <- function(fit, model_name, true_val) {   preds <- posterior_predict(fit)   pred_totals <- rowSums(preds)      est <- mean(pred_totals)   ci <- quantile(pred_totals, c(0.025, 0.975))      data.frame(     model = model_name,     true_deaths = true_val,     est_deaths = est,     ci_lower = ci[1],     ci_upper = ci[2],     bias = est - true_val,     rel_bias_pct = 100 * (est - true_val) / true_val,     coverage = as.integer(ci[1] <= true_val & true_val <= ci[2]),     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   ) }  metrics_table <- bind_rows(   get_metrics(fit_cc, "Complete Case", true_total),   get_metrics(fit_ipw, "IPW", true_total),   get_metrics(fit_oracle, "Oracle", true_total) )  knitr::kable(   metrics_table,   caption = "Model Performance Metrics",   digits = c(0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0) )

code: # Get predictions from each model get_pred_totals <- function(fit, model_name) {   preds <- posterior_predict(fit)   data.frame(     model = model_name,     total_deaths = rowSums(preds)   ) }  pred_draws <- bind_rows(   get_pred_totals(fit_cc, "Complete Case"),   get_pred_totals(fit_ipw, "IPW"),   get_pred_totals(fit_oracle, "Oracle") )  ggplot(pred_draws, aes(x = total_deaths, fill = model)) +   geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +   geom_vline(xintercept = true_total, linetype = "dashed",               linewidth = 1, color = "black") +   annotate("text", x = true_total, y = Inf, label = "Truth",             vjust = 2, hjust = -0.1, fontface = "bold") +   labs(     title = "Posterior Distribution of Total Deaths",     subtitle = "Vertical dashed line shows true total; distributions show model uncertainty",     x = "Estimated Total Deaths",     y = "Density",     fill = "Model"   ) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("Complete Case" = "#e74c3c",                                  "IPW" = "#3498db",                                  "Oracle" = "#2ecc71"))

title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: illustrate kind results can obtained, synthetic example data reflects expected patterns:  visualisations reveal key patterns: Bias (top panel): MCAR, methods show minimal bias (close 0) MAR, complete case shows substantial negative bias IPW better centred MNAR, methods show bias, IPW still less biased complete case Lower observation probability (missingness) leads larger bias Coverage (bottom panel): nominal 95% CI contain truth 95% time IPW generally achieves better coverage complete case MNAR, methods reduced coverage due unresolvable bias
code: # Create example results for visualization (simulated for vignette) set.seed(456) example_results <- expand.grid(   regime = c("MCAR", "MAR", "MNAR"),   pi_base = c(0.5, 0.7, 0.9),   model = c("Complete Case", "IPW") ) %>%   mutate(     # Simulated bias patterns reflecting expected behavior     rel_bias = case_when(       model == "Complete Case" & regime == "MCAR" ~ rnorm(n(), -5, 3),       model == "Complete Case" & regime == "MAR" ~ rnorm(n(), -15, 5),       model == "Complete Case" & regime == "MNAR" ~ rnorm(n(), -25, 7),       model == "IPW" & regime == "MCAR" ~ rnorm(n(), -2, 2),       model == "IPW" & regime == "MAR" ~ rnorm(n(), -5, 3),       model == "IPW" & regime == "MNAR" ~ rnorm(n(), -15, 5)     ),     # Adjust for observation probability (more missingness = more bias)     rel_bias = rel_bias * (1 - pi_base) / 0.5,     coverage = case_when(       model == "IPW" ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.90),       TRUE ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.75)     )   )  # Bias by regime and model p_bias <- ggplot(example_results,                    aes(x = factor(pi_base), y = rel_bias, fill = model)) +   geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +   facet_wrap(~regime) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +   labs(     title = "Relative Bias by Missingness Regime",     subtitle = "IPW reduces bias under MAR; both methods struggle under MNAR",     x = "Baseline Observation Probability",     y = "Relative Bias (%)",     fill = "Model"   ) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("Complete Case" = "#e74c3c", "IPW" = "#3498db"))  # Coverage by regime coverage_summary <- example_results %>%   group_by(regime, model) %>%   summarise(coverage_rate = mean(coverage), .groups = "drop")  p_coverage <- ggplot(coverage_summary,                        aes(x = regime, y = coverage_rate, fill = model)) +   geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", color = "darkgray") +   annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.95, label = "Nominal 95%",             hjust = 0, vjust = -0.5, size = 3) +   labs(     title = "95% CI Coverage Rate",     subtitle = "Good coverage means the CI contains the truth ~95% of the time",     x = "Missingness Regime",     y = "Coverage Rate",     fill = "Model"   ) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("Complete Case" = "#e74c3c", "IPW" = "#3498db")) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1))  p_bias / p_coverage

title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: simulation study demonstrates several important points mortality estimation crisis settings: Complete case analysis introduces systematic underestimation missingness related crisis severity (MAR/MNAR). affected areas time periods precisely least data. IPW substantially reduces bias MAR assumptions upweighting observations high-missingness contexts. missingness depends observed covariates, IPW can recover unbiased estimates. Coverage generally better maintained IPW, though methods can undercoverage MNAR. Confidence intervals narrow give false precision. missingness means bias — relationship roughly linear. Investing data collection, even partial, pays dividends estimation accuracy.

title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: Based findings, recommend: Always characterise missingness patterns data choosing analysis method. Plot missingness rates time, location, variable understand mechanism. Use IPW missingness plausibly related observed crisis indicators. likely case conflict settings data collection tracks access security conditions. Conduct sensitivity analyses MNAR assumptions using varying values ω\omega. Report range estimates rather single point estimate. Combine multiple data sources possible. Different sources may different missingness patterns, triangulation can improve robustness.

title: nurah: Simulating and Estimating Indirect Mortality in Crisis Settings
text: simulation study several limitations: simulation assumes correctly specified DAG. practice, true causal structure unknown may differ assumed model. IPW requires correct specification observation model. incorrectly model drives missingness, weights wrong. Multiple imputation (alternative IPW) may perform better many predictors available. current implementation focuses IPW, imputation options available. Real-world missingness patterns may complex three regimes modelled . Missingness may clustered, time-varying, involve combinations mechanisms. Computational constraints limit number scenarios replicates can explored. Production-scale studies use high-performance computing.

title: Fit DAG-based Bayesian Hierarchical Model — fit_dag
code: fit_dag(   data,   dag,   outcome,   spatial_levels = NULL,   complete_case = FALSE,   missing_method = c("ipw", "imputation"),   ipw_formula = NULL,   ipw_stabilize = TRUE,   n_imputations = 5,   priors = NULL,   chains = 4,   cores = getOption("mc.cores", 1),   iter = 2000,   warmup = floor(iter/2),   seed = NULL,   control = list(),   formula_override = NULL,   family = gaussian(),   offset_col = NULL,   ... )

title: Fit DAG-based Bayesian Hierarchical Model — fit_dag
text: data data frame observed data. Must contain outcome variable predictor grouping variables. dag nurah_dag object defining causal structure. DAG used extract direct causes outcome fixed-effect predictors. outcome Name (string) dependent variable data model. spatial_levels Character vector spatial grouping variables hierarchical random effects. complete_case Logical. TRUE, drop rows missing values (default behavior pre-update). FALSE (default), handle missingness using IPW imputation. missing_method Method handling missing data complete_case = FALSE. Options: "ipw" (inverse probability weighting), "imputation" (multiple imputation via mice). Default "ipw". ipw_formula Formula observation/missingness model (logistic regression predicting whether row fully observed). NULL missing_method = "ipw", default formula using available non-outcome columns constructed. ipw_stabilize Logical. TRUE, use stabilized IPW weights (recommended). Default TRUE. n_imputations Number imputations missing_method = "imputation". Default 5. priors Optional brms prior specification. NULL, defaults weakly informative priors. chains Number MCMC chains run (defaults 4). cores Number CPU cores use parallel sampling. iter Number iterations per chain (including warmup; default 2000). warmup Number warmup (burn-) iterations per chain. seed Random seed reproducibility. control list control parameters passed Stan. formula_override Optional formula object use model formula. family likelihood family outcome. Defaults gaussian() continuous outcomes. mortality counts, use negbinomial() offset. offset_col Name population/offset column count models. provided, log(offset) added model. ... Additional arguments passed brms::brm().

title: Fit DAG-based Bayesian Hierarchical Model — fit_dag

title: Fit DAG-based Bayesian Hierarchical Model — fit_dag
text: Missingness Handling (NEW): complete_case = FALSE, function implements one two approaches: IPW (Inverse Probability Weighting): Estimates P(fully observed | covariates) using logistic regression, weights observation 1/P outcome model. corrects selection bias MAR (missing random) assumptions. Stabilized weights (recommended) multiply marginal P(observed) reduce variance. Multiple Imputation: Uses mice package generate multiple imputed datasets, fits model , pools results using Rubin's rules.

text: daily_data data frame daily simulation data region. must contain least date column one region identifier columns (e.g., region names codes). also contain columns IDP inflows outflows (single net flow column), unless displacement modeled. initial_population Numeric value vector giving initial population start simulation lowest-level region. single number provided, regions start population. vector provided, either: named vector, names correspond region identifiers daily_data (matching lowest-level region names IDs). unnamed vector length equal number lowest-level regions (order regions appear grouping daily_data region). idp_in_col Name column daily_data representing IDP inflows region. Defaults "idp_in". column found, function interpret data differently (see Details). idp_out_col Name column daily_data representing IDP outflows region. title: Topologically Sort DAG Nodes — topological_sort_dag
code: # Define a simple DAG: A -> B -> C nodes <- c("A", "B", "C") edges <- data.frame(from = c("A", "B"), to = c("B", "C")) params <- data.frame(from = c("A", "B"), to = c("B", "C"), effect_size = c(0.5, 1.0), lag = c(0, 0)) dag_obj <- define_dag(nodes, edges, params) topological_sort_dag(dag_obj) #> [1] "A" "B" "C" #> [1] "A" "B" "C"
